pairs.en: The implosion of the dot-com bubble in the early 2000s reduced household wealth by $6.2 trillion.
pairs.zh: 2000 年代初期，網路泡沫破裂，使家庭財富蒸發 6.2 兆美元。

pairs.en: The end of the housing bubble cut away more than $8 trillion.
pairs.zh: 房市泡沫終結，又抹去了超過 8 兆美元。

pairs.en: It’s hard to overstate how socially devastating financial bubbles can be. They ruin lives.
pairs.zh: 金融泡沫對社會的破壞力，再怎麼強調都不為過。它們會摧毀人生。

pairs.en: Why do these things happen?
pairs.zh: 為什麼這些事會發生？

pairs.en: And why do they keep happening?
pairs.zh: 又為什麼一再發生？

pairs.en: Why can’t we learn our lessons?
pairs.zh: 我們為什麼無法記取教訓？

pairs.en: The common answer here is that people are greedy, and greed is an indelible feature of human nature.
pairs.zh: 常見答案是：人性貪婪，而貪婪是人類本性中無法抹去的特質。

pairs.en: That may be true, and it’s a good enough answer for most. But remember from chapter 1: no one is crazy.
pairs.zh: 這也許是真的，對多數人而言也算足夠好的答案。但請記得第 1 章的內容：沒有人是瘋子。

pairs.en: People make financial decisions they regret, and they often do so with scarce information and without logic.
pairs.zh: 人們會做出令自己後悔的財務決策，而且往往是在資訊匱乏、缺乏邏輯的情況下做出。

pairs.en: But the decisions made sense to them when they were made.
pairs.zh: 然而，在決策當下，對他們而言是合理的。

pairs.en: Blaming bubbles on greed and stopping there misses important lessons about how and why people rationalize what in hindsight look like greedy decisions.
pairs.zh: 若只把泡沫歸咎於貪婪，然後就此打住，就會錯過重要教訓：人們為什麼、又如何合理化那些事後看來十分貪婪的決定。

pairs.en: Part of why bubbles are hard to learn from is that they are not like cancer, where a biopsy gives us a clear warning and diagnosis.
pairs.zh: 泡沫難以讓人從中學習，部分原因是它不像癌症；癌症可以透過切片檢查提供明確警訊與診斷。

pairs.en: They are closer to the rise and fall of a political party, where the outcome is known in hindsight but the cause and blame are never agreed upon.
pairs.zh: 泡沫更像政黨的興衰：事後才知道結果，卻從未有人能對原因與責任達成共識。

pairs.en: Competition for investment returns is fierce, and someone has to own every asset at every point in time.
pairs.zh: 追逐投資報酬的競爭非常激烈，而且每項資產在每個時點都必須有人持有。

pairs.en: That means the mere idea of bubbles will always be controversial, because no one wants to think they own an overvalued asset.
pairs.zh: 這表示，光是「泡沫」這個概念就永遠充滿爭議，因為沒有人想相信自己持有的是高估資產。

pairs.en: In hindsight we’re more likely to point cynical fingers than to learn lessons.
pairs.zh: 事後回顧時，我們更可能冷嘲熱諷地指責他人，而不是吸取教訓。

pairs.en: I don’t think we’ll ever be able to fully explain why bubbles occur.
pairs.zh: 我不認為，我們有一天能完整解釋泡沫為什麼發生。

pairs.en: It’s like asking why wars occur—there are almost always several reasons, many of them conflicting, all of them controversial.
pairs.zh: 這就像問戰爭為什麼發生——幾乎總有好幾個原因，其中許多彼此衝突，而且全都有爭議。

pairs.en: It’s too complicated a subject for simple answers.
pairs.zh: 這個主題太複雜，無法用簡單答案解釋。

pairs.en: But let me propose one reason they happen that both goes overlooked and applies to you personally:
pairs.zh: 但請容我提出一個經常遭忽略、又切身適用於你的成因：

pairs.en: Investors often innocently take cues from other investors who are playing a different game than they are.
pairs.zh: 投資人常在毫無惡意的情況下，參考了那些正在玩不同遊戲的其他投資人。

pairs.en: An idea exists in finance that seems innocent but has done incalculable damage.
pairs.zh: 金融界有一個看似無害，卻造成無法估量傷害的觀念。

pairs.en: It’s the notion that assets have one rational price in a world where investors have different goals and time horizons.
pairs.zh: 那就是：在投資人擁有不同目標與時間範圍的世界裡，資產仍有一個唯一合理價格。

pairs.en: Ask yourself: How much should you pay for Google stock today?
pairs.zh: 問問自己：今天應該用多少錢買進 Google 股票？

pairs.en: The answer depends on who “you” are.
pairs.zh: 答案取決於「你」是誰。

pairs.en: Do you have a 30-year time horizon?
pairs.zh: 你的投資期限是三十年嗎？

pairs.en: Then the smart price to pay involves a sober analysis of Google’s discounted cash flows over the next 30 years.
pairs.zh: 那麼，合理的買進價格，就要根據 Google 未來三十年的折現現金流（discounted cash flow）進行冷靜分析。

pairs.en: Are you looking to cash out within 10 years?
pairs.zh: 你打算在十年內變現嗎？

pairs.en: Then the price to pay can be figured out by an analysis of the tech industry’s potential over the next decade and whether Google management can execute on its vision.
pairs.zh: 那麼，可以分析未來十年科技產業的潛力，以及 Google 管理層能否實現願景，來判斷合適價格。

pairs.en: Are you looking to sell within a year?
pairs.zh: 你想在一年內賣出嗎？

pairs.en: Then pay attention to Google’s current product sales cycles and whether we’ll have a bear market.
pairs.zh: 那就注意 Google 當前產品銷售週期，以及我們是否會遇上空頭市場。

pairs.en: Are you a day trader? Then the smart price to pay is “who cares?”
pairs.zh: 你是當沖交易者嗎？那麼合理價格就是：「誰在乎？」

pairs.en: because you’re just trying to squeeze a few bucks out of whatever happens between now and lunchtime, which can be accomplished at any price.
pairs.zh: 因為你只想從現在到午餐前的任何價格變化中擠出幾塊錢，而無論股價多少，都有可能做到。

pairs.en: When investors have different goals and time horizons—and they do in every asset class—prices that look ridiculous to one person can make sense to another,
pairs.zh: 當投資人有不同目標與時間範圍時——而且每一種資產類別都是如此——某人眼中荒謬的價格，

pairs.en: because the factors those investors pay attention to are different.
pairs.zh: 對另一個人卻可能合理，因為他們關注的因素不同。

pairs.en: Take the dot-com bubble in the 1990s. People can look at Yahoo! stock in 1999 and say “That was crazy!
pairs.zh: 以 1990 年代的網路泡沫為例。人們回頭看 1999 年的雅虎（Yahoo!）股票，會說：「那簡直瘋了！

pairs.en: A zillion times revenue! The valuation made no sense!”
pairs.zh: 股價是營收的億萬倍！估值完全沒有道理！」

pairs.en: But many investors who owned Yahoo! stock in 1999 had time horizons so short that it made sense for them to pay a ridiculous price.
pairs.zh: 然而，1999 年持有雅虎股票的許多投資人，投資期限短到足以讓荒謬價格變得合理。

pairs.en: A day trader could accomplish what they need whether Yahoo! was at $5 a share or $500 a share as long as it moved in the right direction that day. And it did, for years.
pairs.zh: 只要雅虎股價當天朝正確方向移動，不論每股 5 美元或 500 美元，當沖交易者都能達成目的。而且多年來，股價確實朝正確方向前進。

pairs.en: An iron rule of finance is that money chases returns to the greatest extent that it can.
pairs.zh: 金融有一條鐵律：金錢會竭盡所能追逐報酬。

pairs.en: If an asset has momentum—it’s been moving consistently up for a period of time—it’s not crazy for a group of short-term traders to assume it will keep moving up.
pairs.zh: 如果一項資產具有動能——也就是在一段時間內持續上漲——那麼一群短線交易者假設它會繼續上漲，並不瘋狂。

pairs.en: Not indefinitely; just for the short period of time they need it to.
pairs.zh: 他們不是認為它會永遠上漲，只要在自己需要的短時間裡上漲即可。

pairs.en: Momentum attracts short-term traders in a reasonable way.
pairs.zh: 動能以一種合理的方式吸引短線交易者。

pairs.en: Then it’s off to the races.
pairs.zh: 接著，競賽正式開始。

pairs.en: Bubbles form when the momentum of short-term returns attracts enough money that the makeup of investors shifts from mostly long term to mostly short term.
pairs.zh: 當短期報酬的動能吸引足夠資金，使投資人組成從長期為主轉為短期為主時，泡沫便形成了。

pairs.en: That process feeds on itself. As traders push up short-term returns, they attract even more traders.
pairs.zh: 這個過程會自我強化。交易者推升短期報酬後，會吸引更多交易者。

pairs.en: Before long—and it often doesn’t take long—the dominant market price-setters with the most authority are those with shorter time horizons.
pairs.zh: 不久之後——而且通常真的不需多久——市場上最具主導權、最能決定價格的人，就變成投資期限最短的那群人。

pairs.en: Bubbles aren’t so much about valuations rising. That’s just a symptom of something else: time horizons shrinking as more short-term traders enter the playing field.
pairs.zh: 泡沫的核心與其說是估值上升，不如說估值上升只是另一件事的症狀：隨著更多短線交易者進場，市場的時間範圍正不斷縮短。

pairs.en: It’s common to say the dot-com bubble was a time of irrational optimism about the future.
pairs.zh: 人們常說，網路泡沫是一個對未來抱持非理性樂觀的時代。

pairs.en: But one of the most common headlines of that era was announcing record trading volume,
pairs.zh: 但當時最常見的新聞標題之一，是成交量創下新高；

pairs.en: which is what happens when investors are buying and selling in a single day.
pairs.zh: 當投資人在同一天內買進賣出時，就會出現這種現象。

pairs.en: Investors—particularly the ones setting prices—were not thinking about the next 20 years.
pairs.zh: 投資人——尤其是決定價格的那群人——根本不是在思考未來二十年。

pairs.en: The average mutual fund had 120% annual turnover in 1999, meaning they were, at most, thinking about the next eight months. So were the individual investors who bought those mutual funds.
pairs.zh: 1999 年，共同基金的年平均周轉率達 120%，表示它們最多只在思考未來八個月。買進這些共同基金的散戶也是如此。瑪姬・馬哈爾（Maggie Mahar）在《牛市！》（

pairs.en: Maggie Mahar wrote in her book Bull!: By the mid-nineties, the press had replaced annual scorecards with reports that appeared every three months.
pairs.zh: Bull!）一書中寫道：到了 1990 年代中期，媒體已經以每三個月一次的報告，取代年度成績單。

pairs.en: The change spurred investors to chase performance, rushing to buy the funds at the top of the charts,
pairs.zh: 這項變化促使投資人追逐績效，在基金登上排行榜頂端、價格也最昂貴時，

pairs.en: just when they were most expensive.
pairs.zh: 爭相買進。

pairs.en: This was the era of day trading, short-term option contracts, and up-to-the minute market commentary.
pairs.zh: 那是當沖交易、短期選擇權合約與即時市場評論盛行的時代。

pairs.en: It’s not the kind of thing you’d associate with long-term views.
pairs.zh: 這些事物不會讓人聯想到長期觀點。

pairs.en: The same thing happened during the housing bubble of the mid-2000s.
pairs.zh: 2000 年代中期的房市泡沫，也發生了同樣的事。

pairs.en: It’s hard to justify paying $700,000 for a two-bedroom Florida track home to raise your family in for the next 10 years.
pairs.zh: 如果你打算在未來十年養育家庭，要花七十萬美元購買佛羅里達州一間兩房的建案住宅，很難說得通。

pairs.en: But it makes perfect sense if you plan on flipping the home in a few months into a market with rising prices to make a quick profit.
pairs.zh: 但如果你打算幾個月後，在房價上漲的市場裡轉手，迅速賺取利潤，那就完全合理。

pairs.en: Which is exactly what many people were doing during the bubble.
pairs.zh: 而這正是泡沫期間許多人在做的事。

pairs.en: Data from Attom, a company that tracks real estate transactions,
pairs.zh: 追蹤不動產交易的公司 Attom，其資料顯示，

pairs.en: shows the number of houses in America that sold more than once in a 12-month period—they were flipped—rose fivefold during the bubble,
pairs.zh: 在十二個月內出售超過一次——也就是被轉手炒作——的美國房屋數量，

pairs.en: from 20,000 in the first quarter of 2000 to over 100,000 in the first quarter of 2004.
pairs.zh: 在泡沫期間增加五倍：從 2000 年第一季的兩萬間，增至 2004 年第一季超過十萬間。

pairs.en: ⁵⁴ Flipping plunged after the bubble to less than 40,000 per quarter, where it’s roughly remained since.
pairs.zh: ⁵⁴ 泡沫破裂後，每季轉手房屋數驟降到四萬間以下，之後大致維持在這個水準。

pairs.en: Do you think these flippers cared about long-term price-to-rent ratios?
pairs.zh: 你認為這些轉手炒房者在乎長期房價租金比嗎？

pairs.en: Or whether the prices they paid were backed up by long-term income growth? Of course not.
pairs.zh: 或者他們付出的價格，是否有長期所得成長支撐？當然不在乎。

pairs.en: Those numbers weren’t relevant to their game.
pairs.zh: 那些數字與他們玩的遊戲無關。

pairs.en: The only thing that mattered to flippers was that the price of the home would be more next month than it was this month.
pairs.zh: 炒房者唯一在意的，是下個月房價是否高於這個月。

pairs.en: And for many years, it was.
pairs.zh: 而且多年來，答案確實是肯定的。

pairs.en: You can say a lot about these investors.
pairs.zh: 你可以用許多方式形容這些投資人。

pairs.en: You can call them speculators. You can call them irresponsible. You can shake your head at their willingness to take huge risks.
pairs.zh: 你可以稱他們為投機客，也可以說他們不負責任；你可以對他們甘願承擔龐大風險搖頭嘆息。

pairs.en: But I don’t think you can call all of them irrational.
pairs.zh: 但我不認為，你可以說他們每一個人都不理性。

pairs.en: The formation of bubbles isn’t so much about people irrationally participating in long-term investing. They’re about people somewhat rationally moving toward short-term trading to capture momentum that had been feeding on itself.
pairs.zh: 泡沫形成，與其說是人們非理性地參與長期投資，不如說是人們在一定程度上理性地轉向短線交易，企圖捕捉不斷自我強化的動能。

pairs.en: What do you expect people to do when momentum creates a big short-term return potential?
pairs.zh: 當動能創造龐大的短期報酬潛力時，你期待人們怎麼做？

pairs.en: Sit and watch patiently? Never. That’s not how the world works.
pairs.zh: 坐在旁邊耐心觀看嗎？絕不可能。世界不是這樣運作的。

pairs.en: Profits will always be chased.
pairs.zh: 人們永遠會追逐利潤。

pairs.en: And short-term traders operate in an area where the rules governing long-term investing—particularly around valuation—are ignored,
pairs.zh: 短線交易者活躍的領域，會忽略長期投資的規則——尤其是估值——

pairs.en: because they’re irrelevant to the game being played.
pairs.zh: 因為這些規則與他們正在玩的遊戲無關。

pairs.en: That’s where things get interesting, and where the problems begin.
pairs.zh: 事情從這裡開始變得有趣，問題也從這裡開始。

pairs.en: Bubbles do their damage when long-term investors playing one game start taking their cues from those short-term traders playing another.
pairs.zh: 當玩著某一種遊戲的長期投資人，開始向玩另一種遊戲的短線交易者看齊時，泡沫就會造成傷害。

pairs.en: Cisco stock rose 300% in 1999 to $60 per share.
pairs.zh: 1999 年，思科（Cisco）股價上漲 300%，達到每股 60 美元。

pairs.en: At that price the company was valued at $600 billion, which is insane.
pairs.zh: 以這個價格計算，公司估值達六千億美元，簡直瘋狂。

pairs.en: Few actually thought it was worth that much; the day-traders were just having their fun.
pairs.zh: 幾乎沒有人真的認為它值這麼多；當沖客只是在盡情玩樂。

pairs.en: Economist Burton Malkiel once pointed out that Cisco’s implied growth rate at that valuation meant it would become larger than the entire U.S. economy within 20 years.
pairs.zh: 經濟學家伯頓・墨基爾（Burton Malkiel）曾指出，以這項估值隱含的成長率推算，思科會在二十年內變得比整個美國經濟還大。

pairs.en: But if you were a long-term investor in 1999, $60 was the only price available to buy. And many people were buying it at that price.
pairs.zh: 但如果你是 1999 年的長期投資人，60 美元就是市場唯一可買到的價格，而且許多人都在這個價格買進。

pairs.en: So you may have looked around and said to yourself, “Wow, maybe these other investors know something I don’t.”
pairs.zh: 因此，你可能環顧四周，心想：「哇，也許這些投資人知道一些我不知道的事。」

pairs.en: Maybe you went along with it. You even felt smart about it.
pairs.zh: 於是，你可能跟著買了，甚至還覺得自己很聰明。

pairs.en: What you don’t realize is that the traders who were setting the marginal price of the stock were playing a different game than you were.
pairs.zh: 你沒有意識到的是，決定這檔股票邊際價格的交易者，玩的遊戲與你不同。

pairs.en: Sixty dollars a share was a reasonable price for the traders, because they planned on selling the stock before the end of the day,
pairs.zh: 對他們來說，每股 60 美元是合理價格，因為他們打算在當天結束前賣掉股票，

pairs.en: when its price would probably be higher.
pairs.zh: 屆時股價很可能更高。

pairs.en: But sixty dollars was a disaster in the making for you, because you planned on holding shares for the long run.
pairs.zh: 但對你而言，60 美元卻是一場正在醞釀的災難，因為你打算長期持有。

pairs.en: These two investors rarely even know that each other exist. But they’re on the same field, running toward each other.
pairs.zh: 這兩種投資人甚至很少知道彼此的存在，但他們就在同一座場地上，朝彼此迎面衝去。

pairs.en: When their paths blindly collide, someone gets hurt. Many finance and investment decisions are rooted in watching what other people do and either copying them or betting against them.
pairs.zh: 當雙方路徑在毫無察覺的情況下交會，一定有人受傷。許多金融與投資決策，都源自觀察他人怎麼做，然後模仿他們或與他們對作。

pairs.en: But when you don’t know why someone behaves like they do you won’t know how long they’ll continue acting that way,
pairs.zh: 但如果你不知道某人為何如此行動，

pairs.en: what will make them change their mind, or whether they’ll ever learn their lesson.
pairs.zh: 就不會知道他還會持續多久、什麼會使他改變心意，或他是否永遠都學不會教訓。

pairs.en: When a commentator on CNBC says, “You should buy this stock,” keep in mind that they do not know who you are.
pairs.zh: 當 CNBC 的評論員說：「你應該買這檔股票」時，請記住，他們根本不知道你是誰。

pairs.en: Are you a teenager trading for fun? An elderly widow on a limited budget?
pairs.zh: 你是為了好玩而交易的青少年嗎？是預算有限的年長寡婦嗎？

pairs.en: A hedge fund manager trying to shore up your books before the quarter ends?
pairs.zh: 還是想在季末前粉飾帳面的避險基金經理人？

pairs.en: Are we supposed to think those three people have the same priorities,
pairs.zh: 難道我們應該認為，這三個人的優先事項完全相同，

pairs.en: and that whatever level a particular stock is trading at is right for all three of them?
pairs.zh: 而某檔股票無論目前交易在哪個價位，都同樣適合三個人嗎？

pairs.en: It’s crazy.
pairs.zh: 這簡直瘋狂。

pairs.en: It’s hard to grasp that other investors have different goals than we do,
pairs.zh: 我們很難理解其他投資人的目標與自己不同，

pairs.en: because an anchor of psychology is not realizing that rational people can see the world through a different lens than your own.
pairs.zh: 因為心理學的一項根本特性，就是無法意識到：理性的人也可能透過與你不同的視角看世界。

pairs.en: Rising prices persuade all investors in ways the best marketers envy.
pairs.zh: 上漲的價格說服所有投資人的能力，足以讓最優秀的行銷人員羨慕。

pairs.en: They are a drug that can turn value-conscious investors into dewy-eyed optimists,
pairs.zh: 它是一種毒品，可以把重視價值的投資人變成滿眼憧憬的樂觀主義者；

pairs.en: detached from their own reality by the actions of someone playing a different game than they are.
pairs.zh: 他們受到另一群玩不同遊戲的人影響，因而脫離自己的現實。

pairs.en: Being swayed by people playing a different game can also throw off how you think you’re supposed to spend your money.
pairs.zh: 受到玩不同遊戲的人左右，也會扭曲你對自己應該如何花錢的看法。

pairs.en: So much consumer spending, particularly in developed countries, is socially driven:
pairs.zh: 許多消費支出——尤其是在已開發國家——都是由社會因素驅動：

pairs.en: subtly influenced by people you admire, and done because you subtly want people to admire you.
pairs.zh: 你會受到所仰慕之人的微妙影響，而消費的原因，也是你暗自希望別人仰慕你。

pairs.en: But while we can see how much money other people spend on cars, homes, clothes, and vacations, we don’t get to see their goals, worries, and aspirations. A young lawyer aiming to be a partner at a prestigious law firm might need to maintain an appearance that I, a writer who can work in sweatpants, have no need for.
pairs.zh: 然而，我們雖能看見別人在汽車、房屋、衣服與度假上花多少錢，卻看不見他們的目標、憂慮與抱負。一位以成為頂尖律師事務所合夥人為目標的年輕律師，可能必須維持某種外在形象；而我是一名可以穿運動褲工作的作家，完全沒有這種需要。

pairs.en: But when his purchases set my own expectations,
pairs.zh: 但如果他的購物行為設定了我的期待，

pairs.en: I’m wandering down a path of potential disappointment because I’m spending the money without the career boost he’s getting.
pairs.zh: 我就會走上一條可能失望的道路，因為我花了同樣的錢，卻沒有得到他所獲得的職涯助益。

pairs.en: We might not even have different styles. We’re just playing a different game.
pairs.zh: 我們甚至不一定是風格不同，只是玩的遊戲不同而已。

pairs.en: It took me years to figure this out.
pairs.zh: 我花了很多年才明白這件事。

pairs.en: A takeaway here is that few things matter more with money than understanding your own time horizon and not being persuaded by the actions and behaviors of people playing different games than you are.
pairs.zh: 這裡的結論是：對金錢而言，幾乎沒有什麼比理解自己的時間範圍，以及不受那些玩不同遊戲之人的行動與行為說服更重要。

pairs.en: The main thing I can recommend is going out of your way to identify what game you’re playing.
pairs.zh: 我最主要的建議是：刻意辨識自己正在玩哪一種遊戲。

pairs.en: It’s surprising how few of us do. We call everyone investing money “investors” like they’re basketball players, all playing the same game with the same rules.
pairs.zh: 令人驚訝的是，很少有人這麼做。我們把每個投入資金的人都稱為「投資人」，彷彿他們都是籃球員，遵循相同規則、玩著同一場比賽。

pairs.en: When you realize how wrong that notion is you see how vital it is to simply identify what game you’re playing.
pairs.zh: 當你明白這個想法錯得多麼離譜，就會看出，單純辨識自己玩的遊戲有多麼重要。

pairs.en: How I invest my own money is detailed in chapter 20, but years ago I wrote out “I am a passive investor optimistic in the world’s ability to generate real economic growth and I’m confident that over the next 30 years that growth will accrue to my investments.”
pairs.zh: 我會在第 20 章詳述自己如何投資。但多年前，我曾寫下這段話：「我是一名被動投資人，樂觀看待世界創造實質經濟成長的能力，並深信未來三十年裡，這份成長將累積在我的投資之上。」

pairs.en: This might seem quaint,
pairs.zh: 這或許顯得古板，

pairs.en: but once you write that mission statement down you realize everything that’s unrelated to it—what the market did this year,
pairs.zh: 但一旦寫下這份使命宣言，你就會發現，所有與它無關的事情——

pairs.en: or whether we’ll have a recession next year—is part of a game I’m not playing.
pairs.zh: 市場今年表現如何，或明年是否會衰退——都屬於一場我並未參加的遊戲。

pairs.en: So I don’t pay attention to it, and am in no danger of being persuaded by it.
pairs.zh: 因此，我不會關注，也不會有被它說服的危險。

pairs.en: Next, let’s talk about pessimism.
pairs.zh: 接下來，讓我們談談悲觀主義。
